data.title: Bitcoin: Unexpected Portfolio Optimization Benefits
data.description:
It's been approximately three years since we last weighed in on Bitcoin (BTC-USD), where we argued that certain correlations would lead to sustained momentum. Although it has suffered from a few bumps ever since, Bitcoin has delivered index-beating returns, consequently verifying our analysis to a degree.

Today's report approaches Bitcoin from a different angle, whereby we re-assess the cryptocurrency's optics in context of its maturing nature and its portfolio optimization benefits.

Without further delay, here's a summary of what we discovered.

Bitcoin: Price Evaluation & A Maturing 'Asset Class'

Market Commentary

Bitcoin has delivered solid returns in the past year, after slumping during the initial stages of global interest rate increases and the concurrent bear market. Despite the coin's resurgence, a slight decrease has occurred since the turn of the year, likely due to a broad-based decline in risky stocks and a technical correction.

So, looking ahead, what's in-store for Bitcoin? First, let's run through a correlation matrix to establish Bitcoin's correlation parameters.

A time series correlation matrix shows that Bitcoin's correlation to major asset classes has strengthened in recent years with its correlation to the Russell 1000 increasing to 0.62 between 2020 and 2024. However, interestingly enough, Bitcoin's correlation to the dollar has inverted even further, settling at -0.22 for the period between 2020 and 2024; moreover, Bitcoin's correlation to the VIX has stayed negative, at least until 2023. Collectively, these figures make for an interesting analysis due to a potential shift in influencing variables.

Adding to the above, it's shown that Bitcoin's price volatility has diminished in recent years. While still a volatile asset, Bitcoin's current volatility is on-par with certain big tech stocks, which seemed unthinkable a decade ago. In our view, Bitcoin is maturing, which is both statistically evident and qualitatively explainable (elaborated upon later).

Herewith is a rolling volatility correlation to substantiate some of the claims made in the preceding paragraph.

Maturity

Institutional Acceptance

Brian Moynihan spoke earlier this year, stating that “If the rules come in and make it a real thing that you can actually do business with, you’ll find that the banking system will come in hard on the transactional side of it,” - Moynihan's words provide a leading-indicator of Bitcoin's institutional level acceptance, especially given the Trump Administration's (and various other global political parties) pro-Bitcoin stance. Moreover, index funds have capitalized on the demand for Bitcoin as an investable asset vehicle; in our view, indexation could add tremendous gravitational pull.

Furthermore, financial market participants often overlook Bitcoin's transactional value (it's not merely an investable asset). Sure, Bitcoin might not be backed by tax dollars; however, it has tremendous real-world application. Until now, most of the transactional utility has occurred in developed or top-tier emerging nations. However, we think the secret sauce lies in emerging markets where people with volatile currencies might want to utilize their Bitcoin reserves as a means to protect against volatile soft currencies - I speak out of experience.

Potential Proxy For Gold?

I pointed to Bitcoin's negative USD correlation earlier in the article. Investors have historically opted for Gold as a USD hedge, especially whenever tail risk emerged. Although speculative, Bitcoin could be used as a co-reserve alongside gold, providing similar principles with diversification benefits.

Here's a recent state street study discussing how Gold and Bitcoin could arguably be used together in the future: Link Here

Portfolio Optimization

Portfolio optimization is primarily a statistical study emphasizing risk-adjusted return maximization through diversified portfolios. While an observation of standard deviations and correlations might possess limitations in the real world, they provide a valuable guideline to investors with an interest in objective theories as opposed to emotional decision-making.

We selected a few portfolio methods to illustrate Bitcoin's potential application in portfolio optimization. More specifically, we studied heuristic portfolios, including the 60/40 and 1/N. Moreover, we looked at a Global Market Portfolio equivalent, to add width to the analysis by including a broad range of asset classes.

Here's a summary of some of the terms used in the analysis.

  • Sharpe Ratio: A measure of risk-adjusted returns by observing excess returns relative to volatility.
  • Sortino Ratio: Similar to the Sharpe ratio. However, this metric bases its denominator on downside deviation. In addition, it sets its excess return benchmark equivalent to predefined investors' required return instead of a risk-free asset.
  • Information Ratio: Measures excess return relative to tracking error by basing both against a benchmark.
  • Efficient Frontier: A series of optimal portfolios based on investors' desired risk-return appetite.
  • Tangency Portfolio: The point on the efficient frontier that maximizes the selected risk-adjusted return metric.
  • 60/40 Heuristic: A heuristic approach to asset allocation, comprising of 60% stocks and 40% bonds. As investors age, this method often turns into other approaches, such as 120 minus age.
  • 1/N Heuristic: A selection of major asset classes weighted equally to ensure broad-based exposure while minimizing the time and effort related to managing broad-based exposure.
  • Global Market Portfolio: A portfolio that aims to sample all investable assets by market capitalization.
  • ETF Proxies: Thematic vehicle proxies used to sample the underlying asset exposure of each portfolio methodology.

During the testing process, we emphasized the maximum Sharpe Ratios and Information Ratios. Other methodologies can be used; however, portfolio optimization via Sharpe and IR is quite common in practice.

60/40 Heuristic

The 60/40 is probably one of the most common heuristic portfolios, ideally suited to investors who seek practical yet effective solutions. Here's a view of the portfolio's weights, its ETF proxies, as well as portfolio weights for the maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio.

Fund Equal Weight 60/40 Max SR
Vanguard Russell 3000 (VTHR) 50% 60% 100%
Vanguard Total Bond Market (BND) 50% 40% 0%

Source: Author's Work

Here's the regression's results, a discussion follows.

  • A pure equity portfolio maximizes the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios.
  • A pure equity portfolio maximizes expected return.
  • A 50/50 portfolio minimizes maximum drawdowns.

In essence, you'll likely experience superior risk-adjusted returns from a pure equity portfolio. However, market tail risk could be more severe, meaning a lack of asset class diversification might lead to an undesirable maximum drawdown. Thus, allocation would depend on an investor's risk-return preference.

1/N

Here's the data for a 1/N portfolio. Kindly note that the selected major asset classes were at our discretion; investors' definition of major asset classes might differ.

ETF/Fund 1/N Max SR
Vanguard Russell 3000 14.29% 45.39%
Vanguard Total Bond Market 14.29% -
iShares Core MSCI EAFE (IEFA) 14.28% -
Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets (VWO) 14.28% -
iShares iBoxx $ Invmt Grade Corp Bd (LQD) 14.29% -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 14.28% -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 14.29% 54.61%

Source: Author's Work - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

Herewith are the results; a discussion follows.

The results of the 1/N provide novelty in relation to the 60/40; here are the key findings.

  • The Sharpe Ratio and Sortino Ratio are both lower for the 1/N portfolio than for the 60/40 and pure equity portfolios.
  • Despite the 1/N's shortcomings, an altered 1/N shows that we can achieve superior Sharpe and Sortino ratios by restricting certain asset classes from the 1/N.
  • The expected return on the restricted 1/N is slightly lower than the pure equity portfolio, placing it second thus far. However, its maximum drawdown is the lowest of all the portfolios tested until this point-in-time.
  • The maximum Sharpe Ratio achievable provides an unbalance portfolio, significantly overweighing Gold and Stocks while neglecting broader diversification. However, despite its shortcomings, the portfolio's maximum drawdown is lower than the preceding diversified portfolios.

Global Market Portfolio

As with the 1/N, the GMP's allocation can be subjective. For example, asset class representation might be mis-sampled and subject to fluctuation. Furthermore, asset accessibility might be restricted, and proxy ETFs usually have partial correlations to the targeted asset classes as opposed to near perfect correlations.

Before presenting our selected liquid asset weights, I'd like to introduce you to a GMP created by State Street in late 2024. The diagram provides an idea of the GMP's exposure.

Next up, a table showing the portfolio's liquid vehicle weights.

Aside: BIZD was used to represent private credit, which is a distant proxy, yet the closest we could find. Additionally, certain liquid vehicles provide amalgamated exposure to asset classes, meaning their testing weights differ from the GMP's.

ETF/Fund GMP Max SR Max IR
Vanguard Total World Stock (VT) 44.8% 23.34% 61.17%
Vanguard Total International Bond (BNDX) 29.8% 11.67% -
iShares MBS (MBB) 4.8% -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 5.8% -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 4.2% 25% 20.85%
Invesco Global Listed Private Equity (PSP) 3% -
iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust (GSG) 3% 3.57% -
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 2% 6.41% 8.02%
SPDR Blmbg Convert Secs (CWB) 1.5% 25% 9.96%
iShares Short Treasury Bond (SHV) 1.1% 5% -

Source: Author's Work - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

Above are the weights for our GMP portfolio, below are the results; a discussion follows suit.

The initial maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio notably overweighed non-core asset classes. As a result, we configured a high Sharpe Ratio but to the detriment of other metrics. To correct the bias, we added asset constraints, whereby we incorporated a maximum information ratio strategy and weighting restrictions.

Here's a breakdown of the weighting restrictions.

  • Stocks and Bonds capped at 50% of the portfolio's allocatable amount.
  • Other core assets such as Gold and Real Estate capped at 25%.
  • Non-core asset classes capped at 2x their weight in the GMP.

Here are the results we discovered.

  • None of the tested portfolios contest the restricted 1/N max Sharpe Ratio portfolio in terms of Sharpe Ratio.
  • The restricted 1/N portfolio still holds superior expected return.
  • The pure equity portfolio in the 60/40 analysis reigns supreme with regards to expected returns.
  • The maximum drawdown is minimized through the GMP's maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio.

By investing in all investable assets, we see docile numbers. The portfolio can be adjusted to maximize the Sharpe Ratio, which results in lower maximum drawdown and a best-in-class Sortino Ratio. The optimal allocation within this dataset suggests that it might suit low-risk, medium return-seeking investors but fails to deliver optimal risk-adjusted returns.

Adding Bitcoin

Maximizing Metrics Holistically

We tested a theory, which added Bitcoin to the GMP, inclusive of the 60/40, and 1/N heuristic portfolios. Similar to the previous section, we set constraints on maximum allocations to optimize metrics throughout, instead of merely isolating the Sharpe Ratio.

Herewith are the weights that were used.

ETF/Fund GMP Max SR Max IR
Vanguard Total World Stock (VT) 44.8% 33.89% 50%
Vanguard Total International Bond (BNDX) 29.8% 5.91% -
iShares MBS (MBB) 4.8% - -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 5.8% - -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 4.2% 25% 25%
Invesco Global Listed Private Equity (PSP) 3% - 4.39%
iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust (GSG) 3% 6% -
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 2% 4% 4%
SPDR Blmbg Convert Secs (CWB) 1.5% 3% 3%
iShares Short Treasury Bond (SHV) 1.1% 2.2% -
Bitcoin 0% 20% 13.61%

Source: Author In Portfolio Visualizer - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

The results are conveyed in the next diagram, a discussion follows.

As with the GMP test, we applied the maximum Information Ratio portfolio test to supplement the Sharpe Ratio portfolio. Moreover, we applied constraints to the optimization, introducing focus on all the key ratios instead of merely the Sharpe and Information Ratios.

Here are the constraints.

  • Stocks and Bonds capped at 50% of the portfolio's allocatable amount.
  • Other core assets such as Gold and Real Estate capped at 25%.
  • Non-core asset classes capped at 2x their weight in the GMP.
  • Bitcoin capped at 20%.

Here are the results we discovered.

  • We were able to maximize the Sharpe Ratios within both the GMP + Bitcoin's max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios to levels exceeding all preceding tests.
  • We were able to maximize the Sortino Ratios within both the GMP + Bitcoin's max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios to levels exceeding all preceding tests.
  • Aside from maximum drawdown, all metrics outshined the VBIAX ETF (VBIAX), which is an ETF used as a 60/40 portfolio benchmark proxy.
  • The Maximum Drawdowns increased significantly in both the both of the GMP + Bitcoin max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios. However, as illustrated in the next section, lower higher-risk asset exposure, including Bitcoin, lowered maximum drawdown significantly while maintaining high Sharpe Ratios.

Efficient Frontier

The information ratio-based efficient frontier shows that the GMP + Bitcoin portfolio can be used to reach the tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier, which includes Bitcoin exposure of approximately 13.61%.

Aside: The maximum information ratio weights used in the introduction equates to the tangency portfolio weights.

Furthermore, lower risk investors can benefit from Bitcoin exposure. The efficient frontier shows that portfolios with 0.15% active returns, yet positive information ratios load approximately 1.6% of Bitcoin in the portfolio, indicating that Bitcoin has application in lower risk portfolio optimization.

From a volatility relative perspective, the Sharpe Ratio shows that Bitcoin plays a key role in optimization with the tangency portfolio achieved by allocating 13.61% to Bitcoin.

Although optimized for volatility-adjusted returns, the tangency portfolio might onboard more risk than some might be willing to. However, a more docile portfolio with a standard deviation of 7.91% (versus the S&P 500's 33-y average of 15.20%) shows that Bitcoin still plays a material role in achieving annualized returns of above 10%.

Risks

Other Metrics Should Be considered

Only a handful of metrics were considered in the analysis. Although the metrics are salient to risk-return analysis. Additional metrics such as value-at-risk, non-linear correlation coefficients, skewness, and kurtosis might add value to a holistic analysis.

"It's Backed By Nothing"

Many, including Jamie Dimon and Warren Buffett, have argued that Bitcoin is unsustainable. Moreover, many retail investors and mainstream actors tend to argue against Bitcoin as an investment on the basis of it being "backed by nothing." Whether counter-arguments hold validity or not is debatable; however, it would be naive to ignore the opinion of opponents as financial markets comprises of buyers and sellers, not just buyers.

Recent Hacking

To our understanding, initially, it was argued that Bitcoin's novelty was largely due to its black box status, presenting a sense of security to holders. However, as many might be aware, numerous crypto wallets hacking have occurred in recent times, raising question marks about the notion of Bitcoin being a safety zone to privacy-seeking holders.

Historical Returns Change With Time (Conditioning)

The regression was calibrated over a period of less than ten years, largely due to historical data limitations. Although insights are valuable, society, the economy, and financial market behavior changes as time passes, therefore limiting the validity of historical simulation analysis.

Concluding Thoughts

Our portfolio optimization tests suggest that Bitcoin could be used as an instrument to enhance risk-adjusted returns. Although limited in time-frame and dependable on the past, the regression analysis communicates valuable inferences to those interested in adjusting their portfolios to modern financial market behavior.

As for Bitcoin's interim price direction. We acknowledge that its short-term predictability is limited, and therefore we refrained from committing to a short-term price discovery opinion. However, we are in favor of Bitcoin's long-term prospects, given its growing societal acceptance, recent institutional uptake, and its general behavior as an 'asset class' - we retain our 'buy' rating.

Kindly Note: The article was authored and tested throughout the space of a week. Therefore, the data may have altered slightly between the time the report was constructed and published.

data.title: SMH: Falling Like Rain, But It's Time To Embrace The Pain
data.description:
What a ride for semiconductor investors this week, as President Trump sets in motion his reciprocal tariffs, as the US government is determined to remodel the global economic system threatening to upend the globalized trading relationships into one that arguably favors American protectionism.

Yet, Trumpians might argue that such a reordering may have been long overdue, as Trump unleashed a tirade against the so-called "globalists," and it doesn’t seem to matter whether you are considered adversarial or allied to America’s interests, as friends and foes were slapped with reciprocal tariffs that could pave the way for an unprecedented global trade war unseen in the modern era.

Such global ramifications have already sent shockwaves across the US equities markets, and also the dollar, as investors bailed out of even the best companies like the Magnificent Seven, with seemingly little regard about their fundamentals, including some leading semiconductor companies listed in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH).

In just over two months, my previous SMH update seems dated, as we face new emerging risks that may not just embroil the US economy, businesses, and consumers, but also implicate the global economy as they double down on their negotiations to try to make Trump change his mind and reverse or markedly lower the punitive tariffs that he has imposed on their trading relationship.

Would it happen? I believe your guess is as good as mine now, as Trump’s policy whiplash and predilection for making last-minute changes or decisions suggest even the most well-thought-out analysis or forecasts could be “subject to changes” that render them irrelevant at a moment’s notice. Such unpredictability will not just stonewall business decision-making, but also threaten to push back on downstream enterprise spending while threatening to compel consumers to cut back further on discretionary purchases of semiconductor products if they think a recession is imminent.

While the hard data may not yet show the “lagged effects” of Trump’s tariffs percolating through the global economy, the market’s response with sharp selloffs engulfing growth and tech stocks is a stark reminder that investors are taking risks off the table at a pace not seen for some time.

If there is a silver lining per se, I believe it’s that Trump hasn’t updated us (yet) on whether he will impose semi tariffs, or when he intends to do it. I’m not going to try and second guess him as that’s a futile attempt.

Yet, although semiconductor tariffs have yet to be announced, the effects from the wide-ranging and significant tariffs on the global trading ecosystem are expected to hamper the well-oiled semiconductor supply chain that has generally benefited American fabless chipmakers, while they relied on chipmaking, repackaging and contract manufacturing partners in Asia for the lower margin processes.

Hence, we already see intense selling across the semi hardware stocks, while chipmakers and chip designers have also not been spared. Chip equipment suppliers are also in the crosshairs as the market starts to price in an increasing likelihood of recession as a base case, suggesting multiples compression and significant cuts in earnings estimates are now being contemplated by analysts and investors.

As for the semiconductor value chain which has arguably enjoyed a “division of labor” and comparative advantage of sorts (US designs, while Asia produces), that relationship that has arguably defined the semiconductor industry is set to change in a big, big way.

Make no mistake about it. I think something is brewing underneath the surface between the Trump administration and the semiconductor companies, as America moves from offering subsidies through the CHIPS Act (initiated by former President Biden) to using tariffs as the main negotiating leverage to “compel” chipmakers to reshore their production and repackaging capacity in a hurry to fulfill Trump’s vision of restructuring America’s chip value chain.

As a reminder, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) has already given us a prelude to what Trump is looking for when the Taiwanese foundry formalized and announced its decision to invest another $100B in early March, adding to the already massive $65M commitment in the US.

And, the call for TSMC to invest or help Intel foundry stand up proudly again has regained traction, as the media reported TSMC considering investing a 20% stake in Intel Corporation (INTC), which might also involve “sharing some of its chipmaking methods with Intel,” instead of taking a financial stake.

Does that mean Taiwan Semi is contemplating a "technology transfer" or sorts to Intel? Does it mean that Intel can finally untether its loss-making chipmaking division to TSMC, although questions about significant retrenchments with such a deal could foment resistance within the company.

However, with a new CEO on board, the chances of it happening are looking increasingly likely, as Lip-Bu Tan highlighted that he’s keen on shedding “non-core” assets, although it’s not immediately clear what he meant by that. Still, Intel appears to be making constructive progress with its 18A node, suggesting it might have a realistic chance of closing the gap with TSMC’s tech, although yields and production reliability remain uncertain.

Could Taiwan’s 32% reciprocal tariffs rate have played a role in the Taiwanese government possibly helping to broker such a deal, but could also reduce the strength of the “silicon shield” if America “coerces” a bigger shift of more advanced chip manufacturing capacity away from Taiwan.

And how might it affect the earnings profile of TSMC and even its downstream chip designer peers? Coupled with the complexities emanating from Trump’s Liberation Day, I believe the need for NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and its AI peers to drum up support for the AI growth story is increasingly pressing than ever. As the top two constituents (TSMC and Nvidia) in SMH, it’s imperative for investors to pay close attention to their ongoing developments and challenges.

And there are indeed mixed feelings here, I think. While Nvidia shows at its Spring GTC that all seems to be well for Blackwell as it ramps, the significant price declines for its Hopper chips rental have also raised the specter of the perils of getting stuffed with outmoded AI chips, as Nvidia continues its annualized new product generation cadence to maintain its tech edge over its peers.

Hence, mixed signals from Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) recently on its data center build-out have coincided with the IPO of CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), as the tier-two hyperscaler shows that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is reportedly keen to double down on its AI progress to close the gap on the leading AI companies.

Moreover, SoftBank Group Corp.'s (OTCPK:SFTBY) confidence in funding the grand ambitions of OpenAI corroborates that the AI growth inflection doesn’t seem to be petering out. And while Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) Chair Joe Tsai cautioned about an impending AI bubble, Chinese tech companies have continued to place orders with Nvidia for H20 AI chips, demonstrating that we might need to look beyond mere narrative to assess the underlying momentum driving the AI revolution.

However, other exigent headwinds could still dampen investor sentiments in SMH. Aside from the ambiguous chip tariffs, the AI diffusion rules are set to come into effect sometime in May 2025. The Commerce Department hasn’t provided enough clues into anticipated modifications, although we should expect the rules could be tightened to further deny China access to the most cutting-edge AI chips.

Hence, these could also render a dilemma for semi investors thinking about “jumping the gun” to buy more SMH exposure now, even as they navigate a minefield of potential downward earnings revisions that have already dodged the industry for the past few months.

Let me be clear here. There should be little doubt that the selling intensity has eviscerated much optimism, to say the least for semiconductor investors. However, there are also signs of a possible capitulation as SMH re-tests its 52-week lows.

So even though the S&P 500 has not dropped into a bear market, the same cannot be said for semiconductor stocks, as we felt the brunt of the selloff, taking a substantial toll as growth and tech stocks were roiled.

Yet, amid the conspicuous pessimism, a successful re-test here could see dip-buyers returning, and the long-term trend bias for SMH remains skewed to the upside. In other words, unless you believe that there’s a structural downshift in the fundamentals of semiconductor stocks, the opportunity to take a more aggressive posture in SMH may be close.

Of course, catching the falling knife may not be something that all investors are attuned to, but it’s certainly something that I’ve been accustomed to. That being said, if you need further clarification into the bullish reversal tendencies, then, perhaps biding your time for a price action validation could be worthwhile, as a failure at the current levels could entail further downside, suggesting that a recessionary P/E must be contemplated.

While the semiconductor industry’s forward PEG has arguably dropped below 1, the forward growth rates could be de-rated in favor of more conservative forecasts to rein in the previous optimism if the market thinks that Trump is determined not to offer any sorts of concessions to the tariffs, although he has also highlighted he remains open to “phenomenal deals.”

With trading nations arguably still in the negotiation phase, I expect things to happen “fast and furious.” Yet, the selling intensity in SMH has reached feverish depths, suggesting a bottom could form over the next few weeks.

While I don’t have a crystal ball, I believe the opportunity to take further exposure in SMH appears to be apt, and 90% of its exposure is rated with at least a narrow economic moat, corroborating favorably for investors who only want to invest in quality during these turbulent times.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs, Dilution, And Decline: The Bear Case For PDD Holdings
data.description:
It’s evident that China shares have been taking a breather in recent weeks after the relentless early-year rally – especially in the Chinese tech and Internet sectors. The gradual decline in key gauges for the cohort (KWEB/KTEC) has reached as much as -13%+ over the past two weeks. Although it’s yet to look like a structural correction, the downtrend suggests one certain reality – profit-taking is in full swing, especially amidst ongoing tariff negotiations. And with U.S. equities looking more attractive following the first quarter selloff, it’s likely that “someone’s gotta give” from China’s overcrowded recovery rally.

We believe PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) stands out as a particularly vulnerable name against the broad-based volatility observed across Chinese shares in recent weeks. Although the online bargain marketplace operator is often touted as China’s e-commerce “dark horse,” a deeper dive into PDD’s business model and fundamentals may suggest otherwise. From slowing growth to emerging dilution risks and looming tariff uncertainties, PDD stands on a weak foundation amidst shifting investor sentiment.

1. Losing Resonance with Consumers

PDD’s long been known for its competitive advantage in e-commerce due to the platform’s prescient focus on underserved opportunities in rural areas of China. Its “group buy” discounts and bargain deals offered through the local “Pinduoduo” platform have been a boon amongst inherently price sensitive consumers – especially those in lower tier economies across China. And by digitizing day-to-day grocery transactions and their logistics via “Duoduo Maicai,” PDD’s also offered rural farmers a chance to optimally monetize their harvest.

By carving out its niche in the underpenetrated corners of China's e-commerce, PDD’s successfully built a moat for itself. This has been key to preserving its growth against competition from bigger rivals like Alibaba’s (BABA) Taobao and Tmall, and JD.com (JD).

But even with 59% y/y growth delivered in FY24, PDD’s failed to appease investors’ aggressive appetite. The continuous deceleration observed over the past year highlights management’s acknowledgement of rapidly evolving consumer dynamics that appears to be a structural headwind for PDD’s business model. Due to the post-pandemic economic downturn and property slump, Chinese consumers have actually increased their appetite for “quality and personalization,” rather than wasting money on “cheap thrills” that PDD’s long been known for.

And Beijing’s recent policy support for consumption has only worsened the situation for PDD. The trade-in subsidies extended by the central government have so far only benefitted premium goods, such as home appliances and consumer electronics – segments dominated by Alibaba’s Tmall and JD.com. Despite PDD’s efforts in building its premium goods catalogue, the platform’s yet to rid its reputation as a marketplace for bargains that’s typically matched with a “you get what you pay for” low-level customer experience.

It's evident that PDD’s losing the moat it’s built on consumers’ inherent preference for affordability and bargains. And moving away from this legacy business model could require a costly operational overhaul. Despite having already initiated a ¥10 billion (~$1.4 billion) fee reduction program that’s aimed at incentivizing quality improvements across merchants, PDD’s revenue growth has remained in deceleration. This highlights the insufficiency in related investments given the depth of its brand mismatch with consumer preferences.

And to address consumers’ increasing demand for quality goods, the company’s also going to have to dive knee-deep into competition with Alibaba and JD.com’s respective platforms as well. This essentially introduces accelerating growth risks ahead for PDD, which shouldn’t be dismissed.

2. Shaky Margins

Although PDD’s Q4 revenue missed estimates, it had still managed to outperform earnings. This was a consistent trend observed in each of the preceding quarters as well – PDD had outperformed FY24 EPADS estimates by 100%+, despite repeated revenue misses during the year.

But a deeper dive into the drivers would yield deterring revelations. The company’s gross margins had actually deteriorated by two percentage points from the prior year, dropping from 63% in FY23 to 61% in FY24. And the consecutive quarterly earnings outperformances through FY24 have primarily been driven by “below-the-line” items generated outside of core operations, which aren’t sustainable. Specifically, below-the-line items grew multiple times faster than PDD’s pace of revenue expansion and operating leverage improvements in FY24, with the more-than-doubled interest and investment income having been a major driver. This offers limited durability to PDD’s earnings quality, as improvements in below-the-line items do not provide reinforcement to the underlying business’ organic scalability and optimization.

And circling back to observations of gross margin deterioration at PDD, the situation is unlikely to ameliorate anytime soon. In PDD’s latest earnings press release, management had highlighted higher fulfillment and payment processing fees as the key driver of gross margin declines in FY24. This is likely linked to the ¥10 billion fee reduction initiative extended to merchants, which aims at incentivizing them to reinvest cost-savings into higher quality products for end-market consumers. Given the initiative has yet to make an evident impact on its topline, it's likely PDD faces the need for further investments of this sort, which introduces protracted margin compression risks.

3. Tariff Uncertainties

The Trump tariffs are also going to be Temu’s Achilles’ Heel.

Although PDD does not disclose its revenue by platform nor geographic region, the company’s growth in recent years was likely primarily driven by Temu – PDD’s bargain platform overseas, primarily in the U.S. This is corroborated by PDD’s ballooning transaction services segment revenues, which are primarily generated from sales on Temu and Duoduo Maicai.

Since Temu’s launch in September 2022, transaction services revenue has grown by more than six-fold. And given the limited change observed in China’s local groceries demand environment, and PDD’s early-stage expansion to the country’s Western region, it’s unlikely that Duoduo Maicai’s been a key growth driver for the segment. This suggests Temu’s been the primary growth driver for PDD in recent years, which makes the company more prone to adverse implications of the ongoing trade war imposed by the U.S.

Specifically, upcoming changes to the “de minimis” exemption is expected to levy a harder impact on PDD than its other e-commerce peers like Alibaba. This is largely due to PDD’s outsized U.S. revenue exposure through Temu. Beginning May 2, any shipment from China to the U.S. that’s valued at or under $800 will be subject to either a 30% levy or a per-item charge of $25 (upped to $50 after June 1). This will inevitably weaken Temu’s accretive impact on PDD’s financials going forward. With the average order value on Temu currently estimated at just under $40, the upcoming charges are likely to deter purchases significantly.

And although PDD’s sought to mitigate the impact by setting up inventory in U.S. warehouses, it remains exposed to the aggressive tariffs that Trump’s announced earlier this week. Specifically, Trump’s announced a 54% tariff on Chinese imports on Liberation Day. The figure consists of a 20% levy imposed in March plus another 34% reciprocal top-up. And the outlook for relief appears bleak, given China’s just followed suit in retaliation by slapping a 34% levy on all U.S. imports starting April 10.

As a result, we remain incrementally cautious over compounding growth and margin pressures, given significant disruptions to PDD’s business model both at home and overseas.

4. Underappreciated Share Dilution Risk

Another overlooked, yet imminent, risk facing PDD is the looming maturity of ¥5.3 billion in convertible bonds due in December.

Recall that the company had issued $2 billion 0.00% convertible senior notes in November 2020. While the notes are set to mature on December 1, 2025, certain holders have elected to redeem about 63% of the outstanding amount for cash on December 1, 2023. This leaves about $750 million in convertible notes due coming in December at a conversion rate of 5.2459 ADS per $1,000 principal amount.

If converted, this could result in the issuance of around four million new American Depository Shares (“ADS”). This may seem negligible against PDD’s ~1.5 million diluted ADS count. But in a market environment that’s already strained by profit-taking and macroeconomic uncertainty, even nominal dilution can have an outsized impact on investor sentiment and confidence in the stock – especially given PDD’s underlying fundamental weakness.

Another concern is that PDD has yet to disclose any anti-dilution mechanisms, such as capped calls, for its outstanding convertible notes. This leaves limited mitigation against the impact on existing shareholders, which means if conversion occurs, the dilution will be felt in full. While PDD does have the cash to redeem the convertible notes when they become due, the ensuing reduction to its checkbook would inevitably dent its valuation as well. Either way, this is an imminent downside risk to consider for existing and prospective PDD investors.

Valuation Considerations

Admittedly, PDD appears to be trading at a deep discount to peers given its outsized (albeit decelerating) pace of growth. The stock is currently valued at about 2.6x NTM sales, which is expected to grow at 21%. That’s only a slight premium to Alibaba’s 2.1x, which is priced on NTM revenue growth of a mere 6%. But when considering an apples-to-apples comparison against Chinese e-commerce pureplay JD.com, PDD’s trading at an almost 9x premium versus its rival’s 0.3x NTM sales priced on ~8% FY25 growth. This triangular comparison suggests that Alibaba may be priced at a premium given its leading participation in higher growth AI opportunities; and PDD’s likely already trading at a level representative of investors’ appetite for its underlying operational risks.

This is consistent with observations in valuation multiples that depend on earnings and cash flow projections. Specifically, PDD is currently trading at about 6.8x NTM EV/EBITDA and 9.9x NTM earnings. This compares to 9.0x and 13.5x, respectively, at Alibaba, and 6.3x and 8.7x, respectively, at JD.com. The modest premium over JD.com and discount to Alibaba is likely attributable to PDD’s relatively higher risks of margin decay and elevated earnings dependence on unsustainable below-the-line items.

Conclusion

On the surface, PDD’s modest value appreciation this year may look like an underappreciated growth story. But it’s evident that PDD’s tripped up in a series of emerging fundamental headwinds that’ll be challenging to fix. The structural challenge facing PDD’s business model leaves little near-term respite for its topline. Meanwhile, the consistent pace of earnings expansion observed in FY24 shows limited durability, as operational costs increase in the face of challenged growth. And on top of the operational challenges, PDD also faces external tariff threats and share dilution risks.

Although PDD’s trading at a relative discount to peers with a similar growth profile, the company exhibits limited catalyst in sight that’d be capable of narrowing the gap. It’s also exposed to impending downside risks that could pressure valuations. With U.S. tech valuations starting to look more reasonably priced in the post-Q1 selloff, and signs of global investors recalibrating their China risk exposure, PDD seems vulnerable. Since fundamentals always take precedence in determining stock valuations, PDD’s underlying weakness represents a threat that shouldn’t be overlooked.

data.title: CoreWeave: Luck Won't Cut It - Reiterate Sell
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWV) is up roughly 42% over the past five days, taking its share price from $37.90 at the IPO launch to $53.80 at current levels. The stock price movement tells us a story:

  • Friday, when CoreWeave hit the stock exchange, the stock didn't move much.
  • Come Monday, it crashed roughly 7% on panic around their high debt burden of $8B and their business model of renting GPUs as a service with a fleet of GPUs largely made up of last-generation Hopper.
  • Then, Tuesday through Wednesday, the stock skyrocketed, closing 46% higher by Wednesday the day as investors took Monday's trade downwards as a bargain and OpenAI stepped in with an $11.9B deal that would reduce CoreWeave's reliance on Microsoft.
  • On Wednesday after the bell, Trump's reciprocal tariffs came out and brought a lot more concern into the stock, causing CoreWeave stock price to drop 12% on Thursday (ouch!).

Now, CoreWeave has still been up double digits over the past five days, and a lot of that is on the bank of hopes for revenue diversification away from Microsoft and investor belief that they got into the AI darling at a bargain. We still think this name is still a sell and recommend investors look for exit points even after Thursday's market crash because we expect things to get worse before they get better.

Let's break down why the good news that caused the stock to surge isn't that great, in our opinion.

Spicing up revenue per customer with OpenAI and Google entering the game

In our last note on CoreWeave, we touched on three reasons to sell, the third of which was Microsoft pulling back and a general panic of AI spending outpacing demand. Now for a quick refresher: this is especially bad for CoreWeave because Microsoft made up 35% of total revenues in 2023 and 62% in 2024. Now, this is concerning since last week Microsoft pulled out of two data center leases, and it's become even more concerning after news that broke this Wednesday that Microsoft is pulling back data center projects globally and taking a "harder look at its plan to build the server farms to power AI and the cloud." So far, we know the company has halted projects in Indonesia, the UK, Australia, and other areas in the U.S. (Illinois, North Dakota, and Wisconsin).

The more recent news augments our concern over consistent hypergrowth for CoreWeave, and we don't think the deal with OpenAI can salvage things. CoreWeave signed a five-year deal with OpenAI worth around $11.9B earlier in March. The other piece of new information is that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) could be closing a deal with Coreweave to rent out data center servers using Nvidia's Blackwell generation, according to The Information report. We think this is more of a sign that Google is looking into the renting GPUs as a service market than a specific positive for CoreWeave, considering that the report adds that:

Google has spoken to CoreWeave competitors for access to spare GPUs, a further sign that the search giant is constrained when it comes to GPU access.

While this news is positive, with CoreWeave enjoying backing from OpenAI, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and potentially Google, it doesn't mean a home run for the company's financials, which are already in flux. We also think investors shouldn't be as naive to think that CoreWeave is the only player in the game and that customers interested in the rent as a service model won't be multi-sourcing, i.e., using different providers rather than concentrating on one.

CoreWeave is looking at stark competition with Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), which we covered earlier this week, and argued that it is better positioned than CoreWeave in terms of three things: 1. Pricing power as it offers the lowest price in the market since it works directly with ODMs, 2. Diversified revenue (its primary business is GPU as a service, but it also has other legs to stand on: Avride, Toloka, and TripleTen.) 3. Doesn't have the debt headache that CoreWeave does. Now, with this in mind, we think CoreWeave's competitive edge is there, with SemiAnalysis giving it a platinum rating. Still, not enough attention is given to the fact that other viable (and cheaper) alternatives exist, which are also backed by Nvidia for the record.

Bargain at current levels?

It's hard to pinpoint the bottom, or specifically, when the bad news is priced in on stocks like this, considering all the AI-related hype around. Our goal is to buy low and sell high, and after Thursday's 12% drop in stock price, we're a bit late to reiterate sell, but we think the fundamentals still point to trouble in this quarter's print.

Our main negative thesis on CoreWeave has to do with the idea that there is a very high risk of tech obsolescence, with the bulk of the company's 250,000 GPUs being of the Hopper series, i.e., last generation. But there are other risks showing up, one of which is tariffs. CoreWeave is a U.S. company, but part of its growth story is that it's spreading its reach into Europe (which is Nebius' home base). The issue with moving to Europe now has to do with tariffs and the higher expense of tech with substantial tariffs placed on Taiwan.

The tariff wars are also not over after Wednesday, things are just getting started. China just this morning (Friday) announced 34% tariffs on all U.S. goods from April 10. While CoreWeave is cheaper after Thursday's crash, we still don't think this is the end of the downside, particularly considering the stock's high valuation and high debt.

The chart below by the New Constructs research report shows CoreWeave's ROIC versus WACC, which is labeled "unattractive" by the firm for good reason. This metric shows the company's total return on cash invested and is a measure of profitability that correlates to the stock's valuation, which is high with EV/Sales of 18.77x for TTM versus a sector average of 2.71x. If the ROIC is higher than the WACC, then this is a basis for positive economic return, but if the WACC is higher than the ROIC, it is an indicator of a lack of ground to generate such positive economic earnings, which, as shown below is the case for CoreWeave.

We think this is still a stock for short enthusiasts to keep an eye on, and we'd recommend investors who are not positioned in the name to avoid it, considering the higher risk profile that has yet to be fully priced in, even after Thursday's sell-off.

data.title: H & M: Affordable But Unappealing
data.description:
It’s been exactly four years since my initial article on H & M Hennes & Mauritz AB (OTCPK:HNNMY) (OTCPK:HMRZF), and the stock is doing terribly. It’s down over 40 percent, and things are just about to get more complicated.

Sure, sales and profits have grown from the pandemic lows in absolute terms, but the rates have fluctuated widely. Sales, in particular, have disappointed; the most recent financial year ended with a negative change value.

Evidently, the downward trajectory had been anticipated, since the focus shifted more and more to the bottom line. Instead of competing on price — an impossible proposition owing to Chinese retailers — the new strategy is to enhance offerings and experiences, but with a leaner cost structure.

So profitability wise, things look somewhat better. H & M’s margins have risen more or less consistently (even if the operating margin target of 10% for FY2024 failed to materialize). Cost measures have included controlling purchasing expenses and lowering operating spending.

The only type of expense that has been rising is marketing — which is understandable given the Group’s efforts to strengthen brand positioning. Relatedly, investments in stores and online shopping have also increased, in line with the growth strategy.

The latest quarterly results, examined below, are a good approximation of what investors can expect from the company over the near or even the medium term.

Financial update

H & M started FY2025 with a soft Q1 (December to February) which missed the expectations of both analysts and the management. Net sales actually increased by 2% in local currencies but operating profit slid by 42%. Operating margin, as a result, decreased from 3.9% a year ago to just 2.2%.

Then it was reported that March sales added only 1% compared to 4% in FY2024. Reasons cited for the poor quarterly performance had to do mostly with the usual cost factors such as purchasing prices, but also a few exceptional situations like transportation delays in the Red Sea. That, in turn, led to increased inventory and markdowns.

Strategic investments in marketing and store optimization too affected the profitability. And since these expenses are expected to remain elevated for the foreseeable future, it is safe to assume that the financial results will continue to be similarly affected throughout this time.

In all of that, the Group will be supported by a sturdy balance sheet. Debt (SEK14 billion) is more than offset by cash (SEK16 billion), and funding needs are easily covered by cash reserves, which are shored up continually by free cash flow (~200% of earnings).

“External factors”

Retailers are forever complaining about weakness in customer demand, but it rang especially true in the post pandemic world. Unfortunately for them, things are only about to get worse from here.

The US was likely the weakest performer for H & M in the latest quarter, like it was in Q1 2024. The management, naturally, does not wish to give up on the market, hence their intention to turn this underperformance around.

However, their efforts are going to be seriously complicated by the US tariffs. H & M, like other importers from the growing list of impacted nations, will have to raise prices, and that will hardly help change consumers’ minds about the company and its brands.

Of course, the tariff war is not going to bolster consumer confidence either, in the US or anywhere else. The economic uncertainty is reaching its peak, and many fear a global recession is imminent.

Valuation

Compared to four years ago when I put a Hold on H & M, saying it looked fully valued, the stock is obviously much cheaper today. At a P/E of 19.7x, the valuation for OM:HM B is the lowest it has been since 2022. The analysts forecast a small upside, about 8% over the next 12 months.

The 5% dividend is great, but the payout ratio is close to 100% (though the same ratio in cash is a moderate 54%). More importantly, the growth potential is far from certain, and the payment record has been volatile.

Conclusion

The management tried to reassure during the conference call. In Q2, investment commitments would be smaller and thus leave a lesser mark on profitability, they said. On top of that, they claimed some early effects from product improvements, primarily H & M's womenswear. I, for one, do not share their conviction.

The new pricing strategy will be hard to implement in the environment of heightened economic stress that is already setting. Because of that and unabating geopolitical tension, the supply chain is going to become more problematic. It might be years before investments bear real fruit, if at all.

At present, despite a hugely discounted price and a decent dividend, H & M still does not look attractive.

data.title: The Stock Market Warning Of A Recession?
data.description:
A Wall Street axiom states that the stock markets lead the economy by about six months. While not a perfect predictor, the stock market reacts to investor expectations about future corporate earnings, economic activity, interest rates, and inflation. When sentiment shifts due to anticipated weakness in any of these areas, equity prices often decline, reflecting a reassessment of future growth.

This makes sense, given that investors are parsing earnings data and adjusting expectations that lagging economic data may not reflect as of yet. In “Failure At The 200-DMA,“ we noted that we focus on earnings because earnings and forward estimates reflect changes in the stock market’s risk assessment of all other events. To wit:

“Investors often get lost in the media headlines about rising recession risks, debts, deficits, or valuations. While those risks are important, they are terrible for predicting where markets will likely move next. Furthermore, if or when those risks become an issue, the market will begin to reprice for a reduction in forward earnings.“

Historically, significant market downturns have preceded nearly every major U.S. recession. For instance, in early 2000, the peak in the S&P 500 was months before the official recognition in 2001. In the lead-up to the 2008 financial crisis, markets began declining in late 2007. However, even though the recession officially began in December of that year, the declaration was not until December 2008. The COVID-19 crash in early 2020 was another example, with markets collapsing in anticipation of widespread economic shutdowns—well before the economic data caught up.

“The chart below shows the S&P 500 with two dots. The blue dots are when the recession started. The yellow triangle is when the NBER dated the start of the recession. In 9 of 10 instances, the S&P 500 peaked and turned lower before the recognition of a recession.“ – Failure At The 200-DMA

However, it’s important to distinguish between normal market corrections and actual recession signals.

Not Every Correction Is A Warning

While entirely arbitrary, the market has long defined a correction as a decline of 10% or more from recent highs. Such corrections are common and occur about once a year.

During bullish years, corrections happen more often than you think. However, when corrections occur, it is not uncommon to see concerns about a “bear market” rise. However, historically speaking, the stock market increases about 73% of the time. The other 27% of the time, market corrections reverse the excesses of the previous advance.” – Bull Markets Often Have Corrections

The historical median correction is 10%, with a median gain of 13%. Given the frequency of 10% corrections, not every pullback indicated the onset of a recession. However, sometimes they are so we must not dismiss corrections entirely.

Corrections in the stock market can occur due to many short-term factors, such as geopolitical tensions, overvaluation, or shifts in investor sentiment. We are in no short supply of those factors. Today, investors focus on the impact of tariffs, sticky inflation, and elevated valuations. As expected, the stock market correction has already led to a reversal in both sentiment and valuations since valuations are a function of sentiment.

However, those factors are temporary and not necessarily linked to deeper economic issues. In contrast, if a correction deepens into a bear market, as the media defines it, a decline of 20% or more, and is accompanied by deteriorating economic fundamentals, the risk of a recession becomes more pronounced.

A Reversion To Reality

As discussed in “Stock Markets Are Detached From Everything,” market corrections eventually revert prices to underlying economic fundamentals.

“While stock prices can deviate from immediate activity, reversions to actual economic growth eventually occur. Such is because corporate earnings are a function of consumptive spending, corporate investments, imports, and exports. The market disconnect from underlying economic activity is due to psychology. Such is particularly the case over the last decade, as successive rounds of monetary interventions led investors to believe ‘this time is different.’”

While not precise, a correlation between the stock market and economic activity does remain. For example, in 2000 and again in 2008, corporate earnings contracted by 54% and 88%, respectively, as economic growth declined. Such was despite calls for never-ending earnings growth before both previous contractions. As earnings disappointed, stock prices adjusted by nearly 50% to realign valuations with weaker-than-expected current earnings and slower future earnings growth. However, both events were tied to other exogenous factors (Enron and Lehman failures) that exacerbated the decline.

Why is this important? The chart below notes that when markets and earnings significantly deviate from the long-term growth trend of the economy, they revert to the economy—not the other way around. However, not every reversion is linked to a recession.

As investors, it is crucial to understand that not every stock market correction is a recession warning. However, sometimes they are. The question is what indicators can warn us if the current market correction is “just a correction or a recession warning.”

What To Watch

There is no guaranteed method of determining whether a correction is just a correction or a warning of a recession. Much like Sherlock Holmes, we must follow a trail of clues and make assessments based on the data at hand. Several economic and technical indicators can help confirm whether a market downturn signals a recession.

Economic Warnings

When it comes to economic data, investors must realize that much of the data runs with a significant lag, but some have a high correlation to economic outcomes. For example, inverted yield curves, falling leading economic indicators (LEI), weakening manufacturing and service-sector PMIs, declining corporate earnings guidance, and widening credit spreads are some of the best. When these factors align with a significant market decline, it often points to an economy on the verge of contraction.

There are currently very few economic signals warning of an economic contraction. For example, our Economic Output Composite Index, which comprises more than 100 different data points, including the ISM surveys and Leading Economic Indicators (LEI), is currently in expansionary territory.

Furthermore, if we weigh the ISM Manufacturing and Services Indices to the compensation of the economy, there is no current recessionary warning.

However, the most crucial indicator we watch is credit spreads. Credit spreads are the market’s early warning indicators if a correction will morph into a recessionary bear market. To wit:

“Credit spreads are critical to understanding market sentiment and predicting potential stock market downturns. A credit spread refers to the difference in yield between two bonds of similar maturity but different credit quality. This comparison often involves Treasury bonds (considered risk-free) and corporate bonds (which carry default risk). By observing these spreads, investors can gauge risk appetite in financial markets. Such helps investors identify stress points that often precede stock market corrections.”

The chart below shows the spread between corporate “junk” bonds (BB), often referred to as “high yield,” and the “risk-free” rate of U.S. Treasury bonds. While the yield spreads have ticked up modestly, this is commensurate with a market correction, not a recessionary warning.

However, as noted, all this data runs with a lag, and today’s readings can change tomorrow. This is why investors also need to pay attention to the technical warnings.

Technical Warnings

Several technical indicators warn that the current correction has the potential for something more. As shown, the market has broken below long-term moving averages, reduced market breadth (fewer stocks participating in rallies), and spikes in volatility (as measured by the VIX) often appear before recessions. Those conditions have been met technically, but the economic data does not confirm them —at least not yet.

However, while investor sentiment has declined sharply, cash holdings remain very low. This would likely not be the case if market participants braced for tougher economic times, as we saw in 2000 and 2008.

Conclusion

The stock market’s value as a recession indicator lies in its ability to reflect future expectations. Those future expectations will be reflected in Wall Street’s expectations of future earnings, which are derived from economic activity.

“Looking at forward estimates, while there has been a minor cooling in the previous exuberance, analysts still expect a 16% annualized growth rate in earnings into next year. Unless those estimates begin to reverse sharply, it is unlikely that the current correction will devolve into a deeper corrective cycle.”

Yes, the markets are occasionally prone to false alarms or overreactions. However, the stock market does respond faster than lagging economic data, which is often revised long after the fact. Investors who understand the relationship between market movements and broader economic trends are better positioned to manage risk and make informed decisions.

Rather than react emotionally to every market dip, investors should evaluate whether the sell-off is driven by fundamental weakness or short-term noise. When a broad market decline is accompanied by deteriorating earnings, slowing growth, and weakening economic indicators, the likelihood of a recession increases. In such cases, adopting a more defensive investment strategy, diversifying across asset classes, and maintaining a long-term perspective can help weather economic downturns and preserve capital.

data.title: U.S. Services Sector, An Exporting Powerhouse, At Risk In Trade War
data.description:
The US is the world’s leading exporter of services to the global economy. The services sector is also the dominant source of economic activity in America. Those are strengths, but they’re also vulnerabilities in a global trade war.

A key risk for the American services sector is that raising tariffs virtually ensures that international trade will decline. By some accounts, that’s a plus because the US runs a large trade deficit for manufactured goods. But what’s often overlooked is that the US runs a hefty trade surplus for services, a sector that’s far more important for the US economy.

“In most cases, the end result of tariffs is that it doesn’t solve a trade deficit, it just means you trade less, you import less, you export less, the overall deficit doesn’t typically change,” says Brad Setser, a senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations and a former official at the Treasury Department and the Office of the US Trade Representative in the Biden administration.

On that basis, the US services sector is in the crosshairs in a global trade war. Consider that America consistently runs a large trade surplus in services – roughly $24 billion in net exports in February.

This exporting success has been a key support for the US services sector, which is by far the main engine of growth for the US economy. But that strength becomes a liability in a global trade war – a vulnerability that hasn’t gone unnoticed around the world as governments consider their options on how to respond to the increase in US tariffs.

The European Union is a net importer of US services, a position that gives the EU “plenty of sharp stones to throw,” notes Tobias Gehrke, a senior policy fellow at the European Council on Foreign Relations.

“The real leverage that the Europeans have is ultimately on the services side,” says Mujtaba Rahman, managing director for Europe at the Eurasia Group, a political consultancy. “It will escalate before it de-escalates.”

Adding to the risk for the US services sector is the slowdown in growth in March, which doesn’t yet reflect the blowback from a global trade war. The ISM Services PMI, a survey-based indicator, slowed in March, downshifting to the weakest pace since June 2024. The 50.8 reading for last month is close to the neutral 50 mark, which suggests the sector is flirting with stagnation or worse in the months ahead as headwinds strengthen from the blowback of a global trade war.

Despite all the attention on US manufacturing and White House plans to engineer a revival in the sector, the American economy is much more dependent on the services sector for growth. In other words, the fortunes of the services sector will play a larger role in determining recession risk and the extent of a downturn in the months ahead
